turns.content: I just finished implementing a basic reader using the seqlock crate and I'm wondering if there's a better way to handle this. My current approach works but it feels like I might be missing something about how the writer increments the sequence number.

Hey, your implementation is definitely functional, so no need to panic yet. The key thing you should keep in mind is that seqlock relies on optimistic reads where the reader checks the sequence number before taking a lock. This prevents ABA problems because even if the data changes multiple times between two checks of the same sequence value, the sequence itself will have incremented and changed.
That makes sense but I'm still confused about whether this is truly lock-free or if there are hidden costs I should be aware of when comparing it to a standard mutex approach.

It's fully lock-free since seqlock uses compare-and-swap instructions under the hood without any blocking locks. However, you do pay a small penalty in CPU cycles for checking the sequence number and validating CAS success on every read attempt compared to a simple mutex which just waits until a thread releases it.
Got it so if I'm doing millions of reads per second should I still consider using seqlock over a regular Mutex?
Yes, absolutely. For high-frequency reads where the write rate is relatively low, seqlock offers much better throughput because readers don't block when writers are updating data.
Perfect that's exactly what I needed to know so I can optimize our monitoring tool now thanks!
You're welcome! Just remember that if your writes become very frequent relative to reads, you might want to reconsider the strategy since seqlock shines when reads dominate over writes.

I've read about seqlocks before but still don't fully understand how they avoid the ABA problem without blocking. I know about atomic CAS operations and the sequence number increment, but I'm confused on why this pattern actually works for optimistic reads in Rust.

The key insight is that a seqlock uses two separate sequences: one for readers and one for writers. Writers atomically update their own sequence counter along with the data using an AtomicUsize or similar type, while readers only check if their read sequence matches the current writer sequence before reading the value. This allows concurrent access without locks because the writer can modify state multiple times (ABA scenario) without affecting reader correctness as long as they always compare against the latest writer sequence.
So if I'm doing an optimistic read, I load the data and the seq counter into registers, check if they match, then commit? But what happens if the writer modifies the data between my check and commit?

Exactly. Your optimistic read loads both values using a single atomic operation or separate atomics that ensure visibility, checks if your read sequence equals the current writer sequence, and only proceeds to read the actual value if they match. If the writer modified the data in between your check and read, their sequence number will be different from what you loaded, so your read fails immediately without needing to acquire a lock or retry with backoff.
That makes sense for correctness but I'm wondering about performance implications compared to regular mutex locks. Is there overhead on the writer side too?

Yes, writers incur some overhead because every update requires an atomic increment of their sequence counter plus memory ordering barriers to ensure all previous writes become visible before the new value is stored. However, this is generally cheaper than acquiring and releasing a mutex in hot paths since it avoids lock contention entirely for most reads.
I see. But what about the seqlock crate specifically? Does it handle these details or do I need to implement the sequence counters manually using std::sync::atomic::AtomicUsize?

The seqlock crate provides a high-level abstraction around this pattern but you still need to understand the underlying mechanics to debug issues. It uses AtomicUsize for sequences and ensures proper memory ordering internally, though advanced users can replace it with their own implementation using std::sync::atomic primitives if they need custom behavior.
Thanks for clarifying the atomic nature of this. One more thing - what happens when I try to do a read-modify-write on my sequence counter? Can that be done atomically in Rust without deadlocks?

You cannot safely perform arbitrary read-modify-writes on your own sequence counters outside of the seqlock crate's internal implementation because those operations need specific memory barriers and ordering guarantees. The crate handles this internally using AtomicUsize with proper synchronization, so you should rely on it rather than manually composing CAS loops for sequence management.
Got it. So for my hobby project I should just use the seqlock crate functions directly instead of rolling my own?
Yes, definitely. The crate abstracts away the complex details about memory ordering and atomicity so you can focus on using seqlocks as a drop-in solution for contention-free reads in your Rust code.
Perfect. Now I finally get why this avoids ABA - because the sequence number changes with every write, making stale data detection trivial without locks. This is exactly what I needed to understand.

That's a great summary. The sequence counter acts as a versioning mechanism that naturally invalidates any read taken after a writer update, giving you correctness guarantees without blocking threads or risking ABA issues in your concurrent Rust programs.
